meta.setting: Telegraph pole on a windy ridge road near French Gulch
meta.rdr_clone_target: PS3-era RDR-clone western art, grounded in telegraph repair, storm travel, and fragile news

research_grounding.title: California State Parks Shasta State Historic Park
research_grounding.use: Northern California mining commerce and road-town infrastructure context.

research_grounding.title: NPS Whiskeytown History and Culture
research_grounding.use: Regional settlement, roads, and communication pressure.

image_prompt.subject: a telegraph line rider climbing a pole with a leather belt and pliers while his horse waits below beside a muddy ridge road
image_prompt.scene: windy ridge road under dark clouds; sagging wire, green glass insulators, a coil of line wire, a distant town smoke smudge, and no modern power lines
image_prompt.composition: square vertical composition with pole and rider cutting the frame, horse and mud road anchoring the bottom, and the thin wire stretching into a huge country
image_prompt.camera: low upward angle from horse-height, early-HD texture on pole, sky, and saddle gear
image_prompt.hand_cut_note: Make the wire feel delicate against a huge country, like one thin promise stretched too far.

style_modifiers.medium: PS3-era open-world western game art, early-HD in-engine repair scene
render_traits: slightly simplified rider, horse, and pole silhouettes
modest texture resolution on wood, wire, glass, leather, mud, and sky
hard ambient occlusion under saddle, belt, hands, and pole hardware
baked storm light with small glass highlights
believable 1899 telegraph repair tools and riding gear
height and weather tension without modern infrastructure
style_modifiers.palette: storm gray, wet road umber, pole brown, green glass, dull iron, dark leather, pine shadow, and cold horizon blue.

text_rendering.instructions: No readable tags, signs, labels, or text.

negative_prompt: copy of commercial game characters, missions, towns, or logos

self_check: Reads as PS3-era western game art at square page-card scale.
The rider, pole, wire, insulators, horse, and road are fully readable.
Every visible prop and garment belongs plausibly to 1899 telegraph repair.
The image shows fragile communication and storm pressure without modern infrastructure.
No copied game characters, readable modern text, or inherited non-western symbols.
